questions.stem: An infant has sudden episodes of crying with knees drawn to the chest and passes a stool that looks like currant jelly. Which action should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction.
Give an antidiarrheal and observe for improvement.
Encourage the parent to offer more formula at home.
Document the stool as a common viral finding.

questions.rationale: Colicky pain with knees drawn up and currant jelly stool strongly suggests intussusception. Prompt evaluation is needed because bowel ischemia can develop if the obstruction is not reduced. Supportive home measures do not address the underlying emergency.

distractorRationales.b: Give an antidiarrheal and observe for improvement misses the clinical pattern: Intussusception Currant Jelly pain points to Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage the parent to offer more formula at home misses the clinical pattern: Intussusception Currant Jelly pain points to Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Document the stool as a common viral finding misses the clinical pattern: Intussusception Currant Jelly pain points to Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Currant jelly stool plus episodic pain is a pattern clue for intussusception, not simple gastroenteritis.

questions.stem: A client who recently started haloperidol develops severe muscle rigidity, a temperature of 104 F (40 C), and fluctuating blood pressure. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the antipsychotic and notify the provider about possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Give the next dose early to stabilize behavior.
Apply extra blankets to reduce shivering.
Wait to see if the symptoms improve after sleep.

questions.rationale: Hyperthermia, rigidity, and autonomic instability after antipsychotic therapy are classic clues for neuroleptic malignant syndrome. The triggering drug should be stopped and emergency evaluation begun immediately. Delayed recognition can lead to rhabdomyolysis, renal injury, and death.

distractorRationales.b: Give the next dose early to stabilize behavior misses the clinical pattern: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome renal points to Hold the antipsychotic and notify the provider about possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Apply extra blankets to reduce shivering misses the clinical pattern: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome renal points to Hold the antipsychotic and notify the provider about possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Wait to see if the symptoms improve after sleep misses the clinical pattern: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome renal points to Hold the antipsychotic and notify the provider about possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Rigidity plus high fever after an antipsychotic is a stop-the-drug emergency pattern.

questions.stem: A newborn of a client with gestational diabetes is jittery, weakly feeding, and has a bedside glucose of 32 mg/dL. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Follow the newborn hypoglycemia protocol and feed or treat immediately.
Reassure the parent that jitteriness is normal for all newborns.
Delay intervention until the next scheduled assessment.
Place the newborn in direct sunlight to stimulate activity.

questions.rationale: Jitteriness and poor feeding with a very low glucose level are urgent clues for neonatal hypoglycemia. Early treatment prevents seizures and neurologic injury. Waiting or dismissing the signs can delay needed stabilization.

distractorRationales.b: Reassure the parent that jitteriness is normal for all newborns misses the clinical pattern: Newborn Hypoglycemia Jitteriness hypoglycemia points to Follow the newborn hypoglycemia protocol and feed or treat immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: Delay intervention until the next scheduled assessment misses the clinical pattern: Newborn Hypoglycemia Jitteriness hypoglycemia points to Follow the newborn hypoglycemia protocol and feed or treat immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: Place the newborn in direct sunlight to stimulate activity misses the clinical pattern: Newborn Hypoglycemia Jitteriness hypoglycemia points to Follow the newborn hypoglycemia protocol and feed or treat immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Newborn jitteriness is not just a detail; paired with low glucose it is the clue that should trigger immediate treatment.

questions.stem: A pregnant client at 35 weeks has sudden abdominal pain, dark vaginal bleeding, and a firm tender uterus. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Apply external fetal monitoring and notify the provider immediately.
Perform a vaginal examination to check cervical dilation.
Encourage the client to ambulate to ease discomfort.
Document the findings as expected Braxton Hicks contractions.

questions.rationale: Painful bleeding with a rigid, tender uterus suggests placental abruption. The nurse should monitor maternal-fetal status and escalate immediately because abruptions can rapidly compromise perfusion. Vaginal examination and delay can worsen an already unstable situation.

distractorRationales.b: Perform a vaginal examination to check cervical dilation misses the clinical pattern: Placental Abruption Pain perfusion points to Apply external fetal monitoring and notify the provider immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage the client to ambulate to ease discomfort misses the clinical pattern: Placental Abruption Pain perfusion points to Apply external fetal monitoring and notify the provider immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Document the findings as expected Braxton Hicks contractions misses the clinical pattern: Placental Abruption Pain perfusion points to Apply external fetal monitoring and notify the provider immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Pain plus dark bleeding and uterine tenderness is the clue cluster for placental abruption, not previa.

questions.stem: A client 2 days after surgery suddenly becomes short of breath, anxious, and tachycardic, with oxygen saturation 84% on room air. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism.
Encourage the client to use the incentive spirometer and rest.
Document postoperative anxiety and reassess later.
Give oral fluids and wait for the next vital signs check.

questions.rationale: Sudden dyspnea, hypoxemia, tachycardia, and anxiety after surgery suggest pulmonary embolism until proven otherwise. Immediate oxygenation support and escalation are the priorities. Reassurance alone would miss a time-sensitive life threat.

distractorRationales.b: Encourage the client to use the incentive spirometer and rest misses the clinical pattern: Pulmonary Embolism Post Op oxygen points to Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Document postoperative anxiety and reassess later misses the clinical pattern: Pulmonary Embolism Post Op oxygen points to Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Give oral fluids and wait for the next vital signs check misses the clinical pattern: Pulmonary Embolism Post Op oxygen points to Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Sudden postoperative hypoxia and tachycardia should trigger pulmonary embolism thinking right away.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old infant has forceful projectile vomiting after feedings and remains hungry afterward. Which finding would the nurse expect?

options.text: A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen.
Bilious green emesis with abdominal distention.
Bloody diarrhea with lethargy.
A wet cough that worsens while feeding.

questions.rationale: Projectile nonbilious vomiting with persistent hunger is a classic clue pattern for pyloric stenosis. An olive-shaped mass may be palpable. The other findings point toward different gastrointestinal or feeding problems.

distractorRationales.b: Bilious green emesis with abdominal distention misses the clinical pattern: Pyloric Stenosis Projectile Vomiting safety points to A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.c: Bloody diarrhea with lethargy misses the clinical pattern: Pyloric Stenosis Projectile Vomiting safety points to A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.d: A wet cough that worsens while feeding misses the clinical pattern: Pyloric Stenosis Projectile Vomiting safety points to A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.

questions.takeaway: Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes.

questions.stem: A client taking sertraline started a new migraine medication and now has agitation, diarrhea, fever, and hyperreflexia. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome.
Give the next antidepressant dose to prevent withdrawal.
Encourage caffeine to improve alertness.
Document the symptoms as expected anxiety.

questions.rationale: Agitation, hyperreflexia, fever, and diarrhea after interacting serotonergic drugs suggest serotonin syndrome. The nurse should stop further serotonergic exposure and escalate care immediately. Delayed recognition can lead to worsening instability.

distractorRationales.b: Give the next antidepressant dose to prevent withdrawal misses the clinical pattern: Serotonin Syndrome serotonin points to Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage caffeine to improve alertness misses the clinical pattern: Serotonin Syndrome serotonin points to Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Document the symptoms as expected anxiety misses the clinical pattern: Serotonin Syndrome serotonin points to Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Hyperreflexia and fever after serotonergic drug overlap is a clue pattern that should trigger serotonin syndrome thinking fast.

questions.stem: A client with sodium 118 mEq/L becomes confused and then has a seizure. Which action should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Prepare for hypertonic saline administration and seizure precautions.
Give free water to replace the sodium loss.
Encourage the client to ambulate to reduce confusion.
Delay intervention until another sodium level is drawn.

questions.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia with neurologic signs is an emergency. The nurse should anticipate controlled correction and seizure management rather than free water or delay. The key clue is not just the lab value, but the combination of low sodium plus acute neurologic deterioration.

distractorRationales.b: Give free water to replace the sodium loss misses the clinical pattern: Severe Hyponatremia Seizure sodium points to Prepare for hypertonic saline administration and seizure precautions, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage the client to ambulate to reduce confusion misses the clinical pattern: Severe Hyponatremia Seizure sodium points to Prepare for hypertonic saline administration and seizure precautions, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Delay intervention until another sodium level is drawn misses the clinical pattern: Severe Hyponatremia Seizure sodium points to Prepare for hypertonic saline administration and seizure precautions, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: When low sodium is paired with confusion or seizure, the student should move immediately toward emergency correction thinking.

questions.stem: A client has been actively seizing for more than 5 minutes despite airway positioning and oxygen. Which medication should the nurse expect to administer first?

options.text: An IV benzodiazepine such as lorazepam.
An oral antiepileptic when the seizure stops.
A stimulant to improve alertness.
No medication until a head CT is completed.

questions.rationale: Status epilepticus requires rapid medication treatment after immediate airway and safety support. IV benzodiazepines are first-line for stopping active seizures. Waiting for imaging or oral therapy delays the intervention that actually stops the seizure.

distractorRationales.b: An oral antiepileptic when the seizure stops misses the clinical pattern: Status Epilepticus Benzo First airway points to An IV benzodiazepine such as lorazepam, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: A stimulant to improve alertness misses the clinical pattern: Status Epilepticus Benzo First airway points to An IV benzodiazepine such as lorazepam, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: No medication until a head CT is completed misses the clinical pattern: Status Epilepticus Benzo First airway points to An IV benzodiazepine such as lorazepam, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Past the 5-minute mark, seizure care shifts from watchful support to benzodiazepine-first emergency treatment.

questions.stem: A laboring client receives an epidural and 5 minutes later becomes pale and dizzy. Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate.
Encourage the client to push with the next contraction.
Lower the head of the bed and leave the client flat.
Document an expected epidural response and reassess later.

questions.rationale: Maternal hypotension after epidural analgesia can reduce uteroplacental perfusion and trigger late decelerations. The nurse should reposition the client and support circulating volume immediately. Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.b: Encourage the client to push with the next contraction misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Lower the head of the bed and leave the client flat misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Document an expected epidural response and reassess later misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Maternal hypotension after epidural should be corrected quickly to restore fetal perfusion.

questions.stem: A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate.
Encourage the client to increase oral fluids.
Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet.
Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours.

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes places the client at immediate risk for lethal dysrhythmias. IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane while other therapies work to lower serum potassium. Dietary teaching and delayed reassessment do not address the urgent electrical instability.

distractorRationales.b: Encourage the client to increase oral fluids misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Hyperkalemia plus peaked T waves is a cardiac emergency: stabilize the myocardium first.

questions.stem: A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors, and increasing confusion after several days of diarrhea. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the lithium and notify the provider.
Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset.
Teach the client to double the next dose.
Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further.

questions.rationale: Vomiting, worsening tremor, and confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity. The nurse should stop further doses and notify the provider for prompt evaluation. Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity Dehydration dehydration points to Hold the lithium and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Teach the client to double the next dose misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity Dehydration dehydration points to Hold the lithium and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity Dehydration dehydration points to Hold the lithium and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: GI loss plus confusion and coarse tremor on lithium should be treated as toxicity until proven otherwise.
